title: AI in Project Management
summary: The 2026 exam treats artificial intelligence as part of everyday project work. The exam tests your judgement in using it - not how the tools work.

sections.heading: Why AI is on the exam now
sections.body:
PMI named artificial intelligence as one of the trends driving the 2026 update, so AI shows up inside ordinary scenarios across all three domains - not as a separate topic. You will not be asked how an algorithm works. You will be asked what a project manager should do when AI is involved.

The through-line is simple: AI is a powerful assistant, but the project manager stays accountable for the decision.
sections.tip: Treat AI like a very fast junior analyst: useful for a first draft, never the final authority.

sections.heading: Three ways to apply AI (Automation, Assistant, Augmentation)
sections.body:
PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition addresses AI in an appendix and frames three modes of use:

- Automation - AI performs repetitive, rules-based execution on its own (generating routine reports, updating logs), freeing the team for higher-value work.
- Assistant - AI acts like a junior team member that drafts or proposes while a person reviews and approves before anything is used (a first-draft estimate, status update, or candidate risk list).
- Augmentation - AI enhances a person's own judgement by surfacing options, patterns, and analysis that the manager then weighs and decides.

A rule of thumb: automate execution, augment decisions, but never delegate accountability. The further a use moves from routine execution toward judgement, the more human oversight it needs.
sections.tip: Match the mode to the task: automate the routine, let AI assist on drafts, augment your judgement on decisions - and still own the outcome.

sections.heading: Where AI genuinely helps
sections.body:
AI can accelerate real project work:

- Estimating and scheduling - drafting estimates, spotting schedule risk, suggesting sequences.
- Reporting and communication - summarising status, drafting stakeholder updates.
- Risk and data analysis - surfacing patterns, candidate risks, and anomalies in large data sets.

In each case AI produces an input the project manager evaluates - it speeds up the work, it does not own the outcome.
sections.tip: Favour answers where AI augments the team's analysis, not ones where it replaces the project manager's decision.

sections.heading: You stay accountable - human oversight
sections.body:
Accountability cannot be delegated to a tool. AI output is a recommendation to verify against project context, data, and expert judgement before acting. Blindly implementing an AI-generated estimate, schedule, or risk response - or letting it make a stakeholder decision - is the wrong answer.

If an AI suggestion conflicts with the team's knowledge or the project's reality, investigate the discrepancy rather than defer to the tool.
sections.tip: On the exam, 'act on the AI output immediately' is almost always a trap. 'Validate it, then decide' is the mindset.

sections.heading: Data quality and bias
sections.body:
AI is only as good as the data behind it. Incomplete, outdated, or biased data produces confident-but-wrong output. Before relying on an AI result, the project manager should consider where the data came from, whether it is representative, and what it might be missing.

This matters most for decisions affecting people and stakeholders, where biased data can lead to unfair or non-compliant outcomes.
sections.tip: When an AI result looks surprising, question the data and assumptions first - garbage in, garbage out.

sections.heading: Governance, ethics, privacy, and security
sections.body:
Using AI raises governance questions the project manager must respect:

- Transparency - disclose where AI was used in analysis or deliverables.
- Data privacy and security - don't feed confidential or personal data into tools that aren't approved for it.
- Intellectual property and compliance - follow organizational policy, contracts, and regulations.
- Ethics - align with the PMI Code of Ethics (responsibility, respect, fairness, honesty).

When organizational AI policy exists, follow it; when it doesn't, escalate to establish one rather than improvising.
sections.tip: If a scenario hints at confidential data going into a public AI tool, the safe answer protects privacy and checks policy first.

sections.heading: AI as a risk and a stakeholder concern
sections.body:
AI introduces its own risks - inaccurate output, security exposure, over-reliance, and unclear accountability - that belong in the risk register and response planning. It also creates stakeholder concerns, including fears about job impact and trust in automated decisions.

Manage both the way you would any risk and any stakeholder issue: identify, analyse, plan responses, and engage people openly to build understanding.
sections.tip: Address AI anxiety with engagement and transparency, not by ignoring it or forcing adoption.

source: Aligned with the PMP Examination Content Outline - July 2026 (AI as an emerging trend), PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition, and the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct.
